sentences.text: Oh, the whole family went.
And they took us down to Athens in a-- in a school-- at the suburbs of Athens called [NON-ENGLISH].
And we stay there.
And we went, doing our lives, trying to-- you know, my family wasn't working.
And I was the only one trying to do some-- actually, a black market [LAUGHS] thing to be able to make ends meet at that time.
That was--
Well, I was-- I was-- I was working, really, buying and selling different items.
And I have a lots-- lots of friends also in Athens, that they help-- giving me some support.
And I-- I was able to maintain, you know.
We didn't have to pay rent or anything-- just we had the everyday life going.
So it was-- it was not so-- not so bad.
Oh, they-- there were, I will say, a couple of hundred-- couple of hundred people.
And they came, you know-- Morris Venezia was there with his family.
No.
Danny wasn't there, because they had enough money to be able to-- be able to have a few places to go from here and there.
But we-- we weren't able to-- to have any-- anything, you know, tangible that we'll be able to-- to go and find a place to live.
No.
No, we didn't know anything about what-- what-- what really the fate of the Jewish people there were.
Nobody knew.
Nobody knew.
That-- that's why when we used to go to the synagogue and give our presents, you know, the Germans wanted us to go so often every week, and you get present.
And one day, in-- in March, 1944, 24 of March 1944, they came in.
They surrounded us.
And that was it.
From there, we went direct to-- to Haidari.
No.
I-- I-- I-- I didn't-- I didn't know too much about it, at that time, to get involve in anything.
[SIGHS] Thinking, but you know, thinking wasn't enough at that time.
You had to have the means to do it.
And without means, even people with means to do it, they weren't able to.
It was a-- it was a very treacherous time.
You know, there were people that they were going from-- from Greece to Turkey, and from there to the Middle East.
But these people, you have to substantial amounts of cash or gold to be able to make a project of that kind.
Well, we went one-- one day.
We went to the synagogue to-- to say present, you know, to go there and be-- be-- present yourself there every week.
So at that week, the Germans came in, and they surround the synagogue.
So it's nothing else you could do.
But nobody knew really.
Nobody knew that such a tragic thing will happen at the end.
Everybody knew, OK, so we went to a concentration camp.
We go to another.
A year or two, the-- the war will be over.
Everybody will come back and-- nobody knew.
Nobody knew about The Final Solution.
My family was there, you know.
And I-- the only one I remember was the Venezias were there.
I don't even remember anymore what happened.
They just-- I know that I found myself on that place, Haidari, there, in-- and it was a camp.
They were amassing, you know, to have another transport.
I didn't-- we didn't know.
But it happened on April 2nd.
No.
No, nobody knew.
They were try-- they were trying to surrounding all the Jews.
And there were these two-- a lot of traitors there I knew.
And there were the two people there that the [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], these people, they're very-- very, very bad.
You know, they-- they used anything that they'll-- they will know where is a Jew.
They will take the Germans there.
I don't know why, because, at the end, nobody survive from these people.
They were the last one to die.
I don't-- you know, you-- you go to a camp, and I-- you don't know anymore.
You-- they give you a place to sleep-- and on the floor, whatever it was.
At that time, you don't know what your mind goes through.
And I didn't even pay attention to that.
You know, I was waiting day by day, by day.
When the day came in, they surrounded all of us, and we went to to Poland.
We were put in these transports.
I remember the only thing they-- they put in was the Red Cross was there that to help us with some food, you know.
But we were a hundred and some people in each wagon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember any more what-- how did we do it-- how we went to a-- to do our regular things.
And I don't remember.
The only thing I remember was day and night, the train was going until arriving in Auschwitz.
There were, you know, a lot of sick people.
And I think there were some dead people there arriving at the end.
It was just from the 24th to the 2nd-- not too long-- not too long.
The Germans.
Well, they-- they-- they put you in trucks, you know, just--
I remember my family.
I don't know.
But I know that when we're in the wagon was-- I know we're together with the Venezia family together, the only family that was at that time together.
Yeah, we-- we thought of escaping, but my father was adamant.
He didn't want to-- to have a thing like this.
Then-- it was very difficult, because there was a-- a small window.
There were barbed-- barbed wires there.
I don't know how we could do it.
It was very, very difficult.
It took from the-- I know we arrived there the 11--
--from-- from the 2nd to the 11-- 9 days.
Yeah, it stop.
You know, that-- that wasn't a-- always, they-- they left passages for other trains that are more important.
But we took nine days to go from-- from Athens to-- to Poland.
The only food that-- that we were were the food from the Red Cross.
But I think we weren't even hungry about this.
Everybody was thinking, and thinking, and thinking-- mind goes wild.
I don't know if we had any conversations.
The first thing that I remember is-- are the SS always, schnell!
Schnell!
Schnell!
Right!
And, you know.
We got into line.
And there were-- Mengele was there, making the selection, always with his two fingers.
Most of the fingers went on the-- on the right and-- which is going direct to the crematorium.
On the left was-- he was selecting 10% of the young of the-- every transport. 10% that we say went to work.
The others were direct to the crematorium.
And I knew that after a while.
I really don't remember step by step.
But I can tell you that, there, there's nothing.
After they selected you, it's very fast.
Everything is going fast.
You go there, and then they take you.
You go to-- to the quarantine.
No.
Nothing.
Nothing.
You couldn't see anything anymore.
They went direct to the gas chambers.
I know now, because [LAUGHS] I-- I was there.
I-- I remember I was with the-- I always were with the Venezias together.
And then, Danny was there, you know.
And the Nadjarys were there, different other people.
But we went to the quarantine, where we stayed by the wire.
They give-- they give you a-- you know, you go there, and they start cut your hair.
They put your number.
You know.
Yes, the number one--
Yeah.
The number was 182568.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Oh, my brothers-- one went direct, you know.
What happened to my little brother is that seeing that-- I think he was selected to go to work.
But seeing that the mother and father went to the trucks, so he run with them, and he went direct to the gas chambers.
My older brother, we stay together.
We were all-- all together.
Well, I think that they give you a hot and cold shower.
They give you-- stay waiting for hours on the corridors, you know.
And then they-- they gave you something to wear.
And they assign you to a bunker.
You know, you go to-- to Block 13.
No, first, you go to the quarantine block, where you stay there for-- for a while, for-- it was about 30 days.
We didn't do anything really, you know.
You had a-- they give you a cup of soup for lunch and some margarine with a bread for dinner.
And, I don't know.
The time went by until we found ourselves-- they selected us to go-- to go to-- to work.
And they put us on block 13, which was the Sonderkommando.
No, I don't remember, but there were the-- the-- actually, they came with a list.
I don't remember exactly what happened.
I know I found myself in-- in that block 13, of the Sonderkommando, with a bunch of people with us.
It were about-- were-- I think it were 35 Greeks.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
It were-- were about 150, probably.
Oh, yeah, you were.
You were-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] yeah.
We were in a-- in a camp.
We were in a camp.
The-- actually, the SS, they had nothing too much to do when you were in the camp.
He-- they-- they assign their kapos, you know.
They are the ones that they're really try to-- to put the things in order.
They are the ones.
Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah.
It was-- I don't even remember their names myself.
Always rough.
Treatment is always rough.
You know, you have to be-- first thing in the morning, everything OK, you know.
Especially the people, they really going out to work with a-- with a couple of SS and the kapos, and-- and the-- the dogs. you know Even today, I-- I can't stand to a dog.
I-- I just-- a dog and-- and a policeman, when I see, my heart beats [LAUGHS] very fast.
No.
No.
I-- I don't remember being in touch with anyone except the people that were in-- in the quarantine.
No.
We didn't know anything.
They just-- the only thing I remember when we were in the Sonder's study, first day, Sonderkommando, I see about 25 or 3,000 people coming in.
You know.
They were putting their-- you know, their-- undress, going there.
And the only-- one thing I remember that always the SS were saying is take your shoes and [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, put them together as a pair, and take it in your hand.
And walk in through the corridor.
Come into the-- before going into the gas chamber, you had to leave it.
Somebody was taking them.
Well, my-- my first observations of that is that I saw 25 to 3,000 people going on the-- on the gas chamber.
And they close the doors.
And, you know, then I knew that the SS threw the Zyklon B, you know, from above-- three, four openings.
And when-- you know, it takes about four, five minutes to die.
Except the people that are in front where the gas is coming, there, it takes some out a couple of minutes.
And after-- after that, you know, when-- after 15, 20 minutes-- they open up the thing.
The first thing I see, I saw the people who I saw 15 minutes before alive.
I saw the mothers with their children standing up before-- because the gas chamber will-- will take maybe 500 people.
It used to make 2,500 people.
Everybody standing up.
There was no room for anything else but standing up.
And when you see that, after-- it's, you know, some, there are black and blues from the gas.
It's-- it's something that, I said to myself, how can you-- somebody be able to survive in such an environment?
Well, the first thing they did, they gave us a-- a pair of scissors to start cutting their hair.
Well, where do you go?
You start at one, two, then I have to put my-- my foot on top of somebody's stomach.
And I-- from the gas, I heard a ah!
Big-- so I jump up 100 meters away.
And I said to myself, how can I-- how can I do it?
How can I-- there was a-- a Polish guy there.
Then I say, where is God?
And he says, God?
This who God goes with.
You have to be very strong to survive.
And I said to myself, the only thing I can do-- that's why I don't remember a lot of things-- I said to myself, I am a robot.
And I-- I did just this to my mind.
And I said, from now on, close your eyes and do whatever needs to be done without asking too much.
This is-- this is how I-- we went at least for the first few months.
I-- I didn't know what I was doing.
I didn't know what I was doing.

I couldn't survive by thinking every time that transport were coming, or where that Moll was coming, you know, with a moter- motorcycle at 2:00 in the morning, you know, and seeing this guy-- this guy, Moll, coming in and selecting the most beautiful girls.
And then he was a good marksman and-- and just marks-- marks them in the more sensitive places of their body, you know.
And seeing in front of you who dies and-- how can you?
There is-- there is no comparison about humanity at that.
What-- there is no such a thing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you just clarify for us a little bit who this man Moll was?
Explain to us.
That-- that Moll-- that was a-- a guy that-- he was always-- his left eye was up and down, you know, closing and then opening up.
And he was the guy that was in charge of the crematoriums on the transports.
And all of-- he will come first.
We-- mostly, he will come about 1:00, 2:00 in the morning.
And we knew that behind that was a transport.
Because they put the railroad cars on the steps of the-- of the-- where the chambers to undress was.
You were just in the crematorium.
It was not-- from-- direct, you know.
Everybody was coming direct, you know.
And when I was there, you know, it was the 600,000 people, mostly from Hungary, from Budapest.
And I remember it was 70,000 from Lodz and Holland.
You know, this Holland-- beautiful people.
It's-- it's indescribable, I tell you.
Yeah.
They gave me to cut the hair.
But then-- you know, they-- then, after that-- first of all, you know, when we came in in the crematorium, the oldest-- the streets in the crematorium, outside, were all from the bones.
When we arrive-- when we arrive after awhile, you know, they-- they took out all the bones from, and they pulverized them, all of them.
And from then on, everything that was done, all the ashes were pulverized and sended direct to the Vistula River.
I was very close to the-- to there.
It was-- they had a room where they were undressed.
They had a gas chamber.
They had the upstairs.
We were living upstairs.
There were-- first, downstairs; then you had to-- there was an elevator going on the second floor.
On the second floor were the eight ovens.
And we were put in the second floor after awhile.
And-- and then we went upstair in the second floor and put them in stretchers, and put them in the oven.
And just from the body fat, they didn't have to do anything.
They started the oven, but then, afterwards, the-- the body fat of-- of each person was giving the flames.

Well, you know, they give you all the tools, you know, what they had to-- all the tools to-- you know, every 20 minutes, you have to turn them around, you know, with-- with every two hours, you know-- it takes 30-- 30, 40 minutes to burn 'em.
Yeah, it was a-- there they had some false showers.
And there were a few openings where the-- the Zyklon B, you know, was thrown from the SS.
Of the chamber, I told you, that maybe will take 400, 500 people.
They used to put 2,500 people.
And the-- the doors were automatically closed.
They were-- the doors were like the-- the butcher, this, you know, the very, very good, good doors.
And there was a big-- where you can see-- you-- they were coming from Berlin most of the time, and seeing how the Jews were dying.
And, also, you know, at that time, were also bunkers, not only crematoriums.
Where during the transports of the-- of Hungary, you know, there were-- we were working about two, three shifts.
And there wasn't enough.
The-- the crematorium couldn't burn enough.
So they had 2,000, 3,000 bunkers.
And there were a lot of SS girls, laughing and laughing.
And I-- I couldn't see how a human being could do such a thing.
From time to time-- from time to time.
We were tell them, you know, just in-- in a very few words, you know, that they are going to die.
And there were bringing in-- you know-- they-- they thought that they are coming to-- we were very-- very well nourished, because all the food they were bringing in, we were eating it, actually.
There were the-- mostly in a-- a lot of a-- I remember a lot of sardines in cans, a lot of canned foods.
Also, some cakes, and this-- these type of things, that the people who are leaving the house, you know, they thought that they'll come, and they need some provisions for a few days, or--
Well, they're-- you know, they get flabbergasted.
But not many times we had the opportunity either, you know, to-- to tell them, you know, We were around there.
You know, SS were around.
So they get undressed.
And right away, they were going direct to the chambers.
And since it takes a few minutes to die, you know, then they realized that they were dying, you know.
So you always, you'll find, when you open the doors, a lot of scratches in the walls of blood.
You know, with-- they're going with their fingers, you know, scratching the walls, to be able to get-- get saved someplace.
But there was no way out.
What language would you speak to these people?
How would you communicate?
I don't know, it depends.
You know, we were talking-- we were taking French, Italian, or whatever it was, you know.
Most of the people, when we where there were from Hungarian, Hungarian Jews.
There are lots of them, lots of them.
Well, they-- they-- they gave us some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] canes.
You reverse the cane, and you put it in-- in their-- and you drag 'em out.

Because when the gas-- they get very, very tight, and-- and it takes a long-- a lot of force to be able to drag the bodies from-- from the gas chambers, you know, to put it in the elevator going on the second floor, and then, you know, in stretchers, and in the ovens.
We were enough people there to do it.
We were about 100, I think, in-- in ours.
We were-- there were 1,200 people all together in the Sonderkommando.
With the bunkers, we're here, we're there.
And, you know, we were 100 in mine-- 100 or 150, you know.
So it takes 400 to 600, and the others were around for other things.
So we-- we-- we had enough personnel, but, you know, we were working shifts also.
You needed more people.
We were 35 Greeks.
And at the end, I think, we were left only 26, I think-- more or less.
We will go to the-- just you put them in the ovens.
Two other people, you know, that putting in stretchers, three, four of them in stretchers, and put 'em in the oven.
And actually, they had to take-- put the outside, the women inside the men, because the women have more fat that can-- could burn the-- the bodies.
You put about four.
I think, were-- in each crematorium were eight ovens.
20 to 30 minutes.
So--
Oh, then you know, they take the ashes out, and they-- you pulverize the ashes.
And they get taken.
The trucks come and take 'em to the Vistula River.
Oh, the kapos on the crematorium.
You know, we had only one or two guards there.
It wasn't too many SS.
They're always outside the crematorium.
Every three, four hours there's, you know, about 1/2 a dozen well-equipped, you know, SS just moving around.
But inside the crematorium were only a couple of SS-- two, three SS.
The kapos were doing the job.
That-- they are-- that the job who they had.
